mission.title: Install and Configure RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
mission.description: The RabbitMQ Cluster Operator is a Kubernetes operator designed to deploy and manage RabbitMQ clusters efficiently. It automates the lifecycle management of RabbitMQ, making it ideal for applications requiring reliable messaging services.

steps.title: Install RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
steps.description:
To install the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator, run the following command to apply the operator manifest. This example uses version v2.10.0:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/releases/download/v2.10.0/cluster-operator.yml
```
Verify the operator is running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n rabbitmq-system
```
You should see the operator pod in a Running state.

steps.title: Deploy a RabbitMQ Cluster
steps.description:
Next, deploy a RabbitMQ cluster using the provided example manifest. Run:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/main/docs/examples/hello-world/rabbitmq.yaml
```
Verify the RabbitMQ cluster is running:
```bash
kubectl get rabbitmqclusters
```
You should see your RabbitMQ cluster listed with a status of 'Running'.

steps.title: Post-Install Configuration
steps.description:
To enable monitoring for your RabbitMQ cluster, you can configure Prometheus scraping. Update your RabbitMQ cluster manifest to include:
```yaml
spec:
  metrics:
    enabled: true
```
Then apply the changes:
```bash
kubectl apply -f <your-rabbitmq-cluster-manifest>.yaml
```

uninstall.title: Remove RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
uninstall.description:
To uninstall the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator, first delete the RabbitMQ cluster:
```bash
kubectl delete rabbitmqcluster <your-cluster-name>
```
Then remove the operator:
```bash
kubectl delete -f https://github.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/releases/download/v2.10.0/cluster-operator.yml
```
Finally, clean up the namespace if it was created specifically for the operator:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace rabbitmq-system
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade RabbitMQ Cluster Operator
upgrade.description:
To upgrade the RabbitMQ Cluster Operator from v2.9 to v2.10, first back up your RabbitMQ data and configurations. Then, apply the new operator version:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/rabbitmq/cluster-operator/releases/download/v2.10.0/cluster-operator.yml
```
Verify the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n rabbitmq-system
```
Ensure the operator is running with the new version. If issues arise, you can roll back by reapplying the previous version's manifest.

troubleshooting.title: Operator Pod Not Starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the operator pod is not starting, check the logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <operator-pod-name> -n rabbitmq-system
```
Common issues include insufficient permissions or resource limits. Ensure the service account has the necessary roles.

troubleshooting.title: RabbitMQ Cluster Not Ready
troubleshooting.description:
If the RabbitMQ cluster status is not 'Running', check the events:
```bash
kubectl describe rabbitmqcluster <your-cluster-name>
```
Look for any errors related to configuration or resource allocation.

troubleshooting.title: Metrics Not Available
troubleshooting.description: If metrics are not being scraped, ensure that the metrics section is correctly configured in your RabbitMQ cluster manifest. Check Prometheus logs for any scraping errors.

versionNotes.changes: Improved resource management and added support for RabbitMQ 4.1.3.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old metrics endpoint; use the new metrics configuration.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for custom RabbitMQ configurations and enhanced error handling.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy RabbitMQ versions.

resolution.summary: A successful setup includes a running RabbitMQ Cluster Operator and a RabbitMQ cluster with all pods in the Running state. Monitoring should be enabled and functioning correctly.

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a running Kubernetes cluster and kubectl configured to access it.
